```
def _get_stdlib_whitelist_file():
    script = os.path.abspath(__file__)

    return os.path.join(whitelist_dir, 'stdlib.py')
```

I believe both cases you mention are actually are covered:

  * `extras=['toc']` -> at this point in the code, `self.extras` has been coerced to a dictionary (there's even an assert on line 243)
  * `extras={'toc': {}}` -> this will be caught by the call to `.get()`, which returns 6 if the dictionary doesn't contain the key `depth`.

The check is for the case where the user supplied a list, in which case it will be converted by code above (lines 238/241) to a dict with all values set to `None`, or simply if the user passed `extras={'toc': None}`. Well, are we expecting further major breakages after the rewrite? It's these (premature?) restrictions that cause upgrade troubles for larger projects. It's why we're stuck on an old requests version even though the newest one seems to work just fine. If you use tools that do proper restriction checking (like pip-faster does) then you won't be able to use a newer version. pip doesn't really do sophisticated checking.

I'd vote for removing this restriction if there is no reason for it right now and instead focus on providing a stable API with fido. That's what we should aim for, that's what we should expect. Let's not code for failure. And if we do need to add such a restriction it should be for a major version, i.e. fido < 4.

We had this discussion before and it is just another example how using flake8 and its ilk to enforce the code "quality" makes life more miserable for everyone. Basically it is counterproductive.

Tests kept failing because the buildbot would enforce rules for code not being formatted right, but then when one uses a formatter that is supposedly pep8 aware and makes these errors go away it ends up reformatting the code with rules that flake8 did not enforce. 

By the time of the third attempt to fix failing test that had nothing to do with code quality I lost the focus of ensuring that the changes are minimal and just wanted to shut it up already.

@benjello C'est dans cet idée que je propose la première option, qui est à mon avis au plus proche de l'esprit d'OpenFisca. 
Les + : 
- Plus court donc plus lisible
- pas de copié/collé de code donc plus maintenable
- Respecte le fonctionnement global des inputs utilisateurs qui prennent le pas sur les formules.

Le moins : 
- risque de mauvais résultats si les periodes des inputs ne sont pas respecté par les utilisateurs. Ce qui est le risque de n'importe quelle variable (input ou formule) d'OpenFisca

Ma préconisation est donc :
```py
class grosses_reparations(Variable):
    value_type = float
    entity = FoyerFiscal
    label = u"Dépenses de grosses réparations des nus-propriétaires"
    definition_period = YEAR

    def formula_2009(foyer_fiscal, period, parameters):
        '''
        Dépenses de grosses réparations des nus-propriétaires
        '''
        somme_reparations = (
               foyer_fiscal('f6cb', period) +
               foyer_fiscal('f6cj', period) + # report année 2009
               foyer_fiscal('f6hk', period) +  # report année 2010
               foyer_fiscal('f6hl', period) +  # report année 2011
               foyer_fiscal('f6hm', period) +  # report année 2012
               foyer_fiscal('f6hn', period) +  # report année 2013
               foyer_fiscal('f6ho', period) +  # report année 2014
               foyer_fiscal('f6hp', period)   # report année 2015
        )
   
        plafond_grosses_reparations = parameters(period).impot_revenu.charges_deductibles.grosses_reparations.plafond
```

Unexpected bug in Vue2 here. Prior to adding this line, if you hit `enter` while focused on the submit button for usercreate, the button would fire its `click` event.

Did some research, but most of the [results](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/12325066/button-click-event-fires-when-pressing-enter-key-in-different-input-no-forms) talked about how it had to do with the HTML spec (buttons are submit by default) and descriped how the solution could be curbed by adding a `type="button"` attribute.

This wasn't the case for me. Even after adding that line(4), click event was still firing. Started expirementing by creating buttons inside the modal and listening for click events on the whole document (Devtools are pretty sweet) and saw that it was indeed firing a `click` event on `enter` regardless of whether the attribute was present. This behavior was consistent across Chrome and Firefox.

Side note: same thing happens with the `<select>` element - after picking one with tab and hitting enter, a click event fires. Can test for yourself by typing `monitorEvents(document, "click");` into the console and seeing what elements cause it to fire.

This is only going to work for HVM by default. To work with PV, you'll need to "sysctl -w xen.independent_wallclock=1" in order to allow the PV clock to be settable independently from the underlying Xen host (which is already running NTP, providing the wallclock that the PV sees). PV maps the time to a hypervisor call to ask the underlying host.  HVM, and raw-iron linux clocks, keep track of the time using linux kernel jiffy counters, which can drift wildly when cycles are stolen by other VMs on the same Xen host machine.
If only there was a way we could deploy this with `deisctl`... We'll never be able to tune this in future releases, which could be a concern.

So this `while` loop is not idiomatic. Rather than repeatedly query the `len` and popleft, we can just iterate over the `table_size_changes` container directly and then resize at the end:

```python
for change in self.table_size_changes:
    do_stuff()
self.table_size_changes = collections.deque()
```

Once we make that change we're no longer popping from the left so the advantage of `collections.deque` is gone, so we should also just replace that with a list (cheaper in memory).

There are three options for the `project_vpc_id` property in the HTTP API:
- Setting it to a UUID (a result of using `--project-vpc-id`)
- Setting it to `null` (a result of using `--no-project-vpc`)
- Not defining the property (a result of not using either of the switches)

The `UNDEFINED` constant is used for the third case - a None value leads to the second (`null`), which has a different meaning. This way is backwards compatible.
